coredata.dc:title: Sulfo-modified MIL-101 with immobilized carbon quantum dots as a fluorescence sensing platform for highly sensitive detection of DNP
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Ji-Min
Kou, Yun-Kai
coredata.dc:description: Metal-organic framework-based composites can help overcome the functional shortcomings of single materials by virtue of the synergetic properties of their components. The new functionalities introduced in the resulting material allow for their use in a wide range of applications. Herein, we prepared a fluorescent amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H composite sensor by immobilizing amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots (amino-CQDs) on sulfo-modified MIL-101 nanoparticles. MIL-101-SO3H was not only used as a carrier to encapsulate the amino-CQDs in the pore cavity by hydrogen bonding interactions between the free -SO3H groups of MIL-101-SO3H and the –NH2 group of the amino-CQDs, but also as an adsorbent to selectively adsorb and capture the target analyte, i.e. 2,4-dinitrophenol (DNP), via hydrogen bonding interactions between the free -SO3H groups of MIL-101-SO3H and the –NO2 group of DNP. The amino-CQDs were used as a probe to sense the target molecules with high selectivity. The amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H composite sensor displayed excellent selectivity and sensitivity toward DNP, with a detection limit of 0.041 μM. This is the first study to demonstrate the immobilization of fluorescent amino-CQDs in mesostructured MIL-101-SO3H. The selective fluorescence detection demonstrated herein will provide a new platform for sensing hazardous pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Metal-organic framework
Composite
Fluorescent sensor
2,4-Dinitrophenol

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation, China (Grant No. ZR2018LB05), a project of the Shandong Province Higher Educational Science and Technology Program (Grant No. J16LC04), and the Natural Science Foundation of Linyi University (Grant No. LYDX2016BS072).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of FL amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H composite and its adsorption of DNP.
(a) PXRD pattern of the amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H composite and that simulated from the single-crystal XRD data. (b) FT-IR spectra of MIL-101-SO3H and the amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H composite.
SEM images of (a) MIL-101-SO3H and (b) amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H composite.
Energy-dispersive X-ray mappings of amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H: scanning electron micrograph (top left) and elemental distributions of (b) carbon, (c) oxygen, (d) sulfur, (e) chromium, and (f) nitrogen.
(a) TGA profiles and (b) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of pristine MIL-101-SO3H and the amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H composite.
Selectivity of the amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H composite for DNP, other phenol analogs, and metal ions.

(a) FL emission spectra of amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H in different concentrations of DNP. (b) Linear calibration plot of I 0/I-1 versus DNP concentration (C). Excitation wavelength, 365 nm; slit widths for excitation and emission, 2.5 and 5 nm, respectively.

head.ce:title: Sulfo-modified MIL-101 with immobilized carbon quantum dots as a fluorescence sensing platform for highly sensitive detection of DNP

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Linyi University, Linyi 276005, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Linyi University, Linyi 276005, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We fabricated amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H composites via a post-synthetic modification, loading amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots functional monomers that could respond to DNP. We found it to be highly selective and sensitive for DNP, detecting this species even in mixtures of metal ions and other phenols.

Metal-organic framework-based composites can help overcome the functional shortcomings of single materials by virtue of the synergetic properties of their components. The new functionalities introduced in the resulting material allow for their use in a wide range of applications. Herein, we prepared a fluorescent amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H composite sensor by immobilizing amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots (amino-CQDs) on sulfo-modified MIL-101 nanoparticles. MIL-101-SO3H was not only used as a carrier to encapsulate the amino-CQDs in the pore cavity by hydrogen bonding interactions between the free -SO3H groups of MIL-101-SO3H and the –NH2 group of the amino-CQDs, but also as an adsorbent to selectively adsorb and capture the target analyte, i.e. 2,4-dinitrophenol (DNP), via hydrogen bonding interactions between the free -SO3H groups of MIL-101-SO3H and the –NO2 group of DNP. The amino-CQDs were used as a probe to sense the target molecules with high selectivity. The amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H composite sensor displayed excellent selectivity and sensitivity toward DNP, with a detection limit of 0.041 μM. This is the first study to demonstrate the immobilization of fluorescent amino-CQDs in mesostructured MIL-101-SO3H. The selective fluorescence detection demonstrated herein will provide a new platform for sensing hazardous pollutants.

ce:section.ce:para: Phenol and its derivatives, such as 4-nitrophenol and 2,4-dinitrophenol (DNP), are the most common environmental pollutants in industrial effluents [1,2]. Moreover, nitrophenols are classified as priority pollutants by the US Environmental Protection Agency [3,4]. Phenolic compounds are carcinogenic to humans and animals even at low concentrations. Because of their ultrahigh toxicity, poor biodegradability, and high bioaccumulation, nitrophenols pose a particularly high risk to human and animal health, and are harmful to the environment [5]. Human exposure to phenolic compounds can result in serious health effects, including vomiting, liver and kidney injury, cancer, diarrhea, skin burns, tissue erosion, and nervous system paralysis [1,6].
DNP is widely used in the manufacture of dyes, pesticides, and pharmaceuticals. Therefore, the highly selective and ultrasensitive detection of DNP is imperative from the perspective of human health.

Although various technologies have been developed for the detection of these toxic chemicals [7,8] luminescence-based chemical sensors and methods have attracted considerable scientific interest due to their fast response, high sensitivity, selectivity, and easily detectable signals [9–13]. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have low quantum yields [14] which limits their use in practical fluorescence (FL) sensing applications [15]. Introduction of fluorophores with high quantum yields inside the pore cavity of porous MOFs is a promising alternative approach to fabricate ideal luminescent MOF sensing materials, while maintaining the MOF skeleton and properties. The direct impregnation of fluorophores into MOFs is employed as an important strategy for preparing functionalized MOF composites, in which functional molecules or ions are encapsulated inside the pore cavity of the MOFs. Recently, luminescent MOF sensors have been prepared by immobilizing lanthanide ions (e.g., Eu3+, Ln3+, and Tb3+) into the channels of MOFs [16–19] or by loading quantum dots into the pore cavities of MOF nanoparticles [20–24]. Fabricating luminescent MOF composites with high quantum yields is challenging. At the same time, such composites are necessary for extending their applicability to the highly selective and sensitive sensing of hazardous pollutants. In this regard, fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have received significant attention because of their high photoluminescence stability and quantum yields, low toxicity, and green synthetic routes [25,26].

MIL-101(Cr) is a well-known porous MOF with two types of mesoporous cages and exceptional pore volumes [27]. It has applications in gas storage/separation, pollutant adsorption, and catalysis [28–33]. Although the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area of MIL-101(Cr) is ~ 4000 m2 g−1 [34] it does not adsorb DNP molecules (Fig. S1) due to the weak driving forces for the adsorption of DNP on MIL-101(Cr). Therefore, in this study, we introduced -SO3H groups into the MOF to prepare MIL-101-SO3H, and immobilized amino-functionalized CQDs (amino-CQDs) into its pore channels through hydrogen bonding between the –NH2 groups of the amino-CQDs and -SO3H groups of the MIL-101-SO3H. These modifications enhanced the adsorption capacity and driving force for the adsorption of DNP into the pore cavity via hydrogen bonding between the –NO2 groups of DNP and -SO3H groups of MIL-101-SO3H.

The resulting fluorescent amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H composite, prepared using a facile method, allowed the highly selective and sensitive detection of trace DNP in the concentration range 0.01–40.0 μM, with a detection limit of 4.5 nM. The selectivity and sensitivity were attributable to the -SO3H groups, which played a significant role in sensing DNP. This strategy, involving the use of the amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H composite, has potential applications in the field of pollutant analysis.

2-Sulfoterephthalic acid monosodium salt (H2BDC-SO3Na, 98%) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 2,4-Dinitrophenol (DNP, 98%), trinitrophenol (TNP, 98%), phenol (PHE, 98%), and 4-nitrophenol (4-NP, 98%) were obtained from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Terephthalic acid (H2BDC, 98%), tetramethylammonium hydroxide (TMAOH, 10%), and chromium nitrate nonahydrate [Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, 99%] were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethylenediamine (98%) and citric acid (98%) were bought from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China).

In a typical experiment, H2BDC-SO3Na (37.3 mg), H2BDC (198.0 mg), and TMAOH (63 μL) were dispersed in distilled water (18 mL) and agitated for 0.5 h. Subsequently, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O (500 mg) was dissolved in the suspension. After agitating for 0.5 h, the resulting mixture was placed inside a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 453 K for 24 h. The green precipitate was washed five times with distilled water.

Amino-CQDs were synthesized following a literature procedure.16 Typically, citric acid (0.6 g) and ethylenediamine (6.0 mL) were dissolved in distilled water (20 mL). The resulting mixture was sealed and placed inside a 25-mL Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 473 K for 5 h. Finally, the obtained amino-CQD solution was dialyzed against deionized water for 4 days (molecular weight cut-off of dialysis bag = 1000) at 298 K to remove the unreacted residues.

MIL-101-SO3H microcrystals (50 mg) were immersed in the obtained amino-CQDs solution (1 mL) for 4 h. Then, the sediment was washed three times with deionized water. Finally, the sample was activated at 110 °C for 4 h under vacuum.

X-ray diffraction (PXRD) patterns were collected on a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer using CuKα radiation (wavelength = 1.54 Å). Energy-dispersive spectroscopy and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) were performed on an FEI Quanta 450 FEG scanning electron microscope. Absorbance was measured on a Shimadzu UV-1800 ultraviolet spectrophotometer, and Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were acquired on a Bruker Vector 22 FT-IR spectrophotometer. Thermal stabilities of the as-obtained samples were measured by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) using a TA Q600 thermal analyzer, ramping at 10 °C min−1. N2 sorption experiments were measured on a Micromeritics ASAP 2460 sorptometer at 77 K.

To evaluate the sensing performance of the prepared composite, different amounts of DNP were quickly added to 4.0 mL of the amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H dispersion solution (40 μg mL−1). After incubating 10 min, FL spectra of the above solutions were recorded on a Hitachi FL-7000 spectrophotometer by exciting at 365 nm. Excitation and emission bandwidths were kept at 2.5 and 5.0 nm, respectively.

The amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H composite was prepared in three steps, as shown in Fig. 1 . First, blue fluorescent amino-CQDs were synthesized by a solvothermal method using ethylenediamine and citric acid in deionized water. The amino-CQDs showed strong blue FL with an emission maximum at 439.6 nm when excited at a wavelength of 365 nm (Fig. S2). Second, MIL-101-SO3H microcrystals were prepared by mixing aqueous solutions of Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, H2BDC, H2BDC-SO3Na, and TMAOH. Finally, MIL-101-SO3H was exposed to an aqueous solution of amino-CQDs to form the amino-CQD-incorporated amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H composite. The FL emission spectra of MIL-101-SO3H, amino-CQDs, and amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H are shown in Fig. S3.

The FL emission maxima of the amino-CQDs and amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H almost overlapped at the peak maxima. However, no obvious emission peak at 439.6 nm was observed for MIL-101-SO3H, implying that the blue fluorescence of amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H originated from the amino-CQDs rather than the MIL-101-SO3H microcrystals. It is also evident that the blue fluorescence properties of the amino-CQDs in amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H were maintained. The FL stability of amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H is shown in Fig. S4. The FL intensity remained constant even after storing the composite for 18 days, implying the excellent stability of the prepared fluorescent amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H composite.

TGA curves of the amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H composite and MIL-101-SO3H are shown in Fig. 5 a. The same thermal decomposition temperature for MIL-101-SO3H and amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H indicated the good thermal stability of the amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H composite. However, the weight loss in each step was different. In the first step, the weight loss from amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H was lower than that of pristine MIL-101-SO3H due to the smaller pore volume of the former. In the second step, the residual percentage of the amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H composite was higher than that of MIL-101-SO3H owing to the presence of amino-CQDs. Thus, TGA also confirmed the immobilization of amino-CQDs in the MOF skeleton.

The BET surface area of amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H (2588.3 m2 g−1) was smaller than that of the parent MIL-101-SO3H (2818.1 m2 g−1) [36]; the N2 uptake of the former was also lower than that of the latter (Fig. 5). The pore size distributions (Fig. S5) indicated that the largest channel size of amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H (1.94 nm) was slightly smaller than that of pristine MIL-101-SO3H (2.19 nm). This is possibly because the amino-CQDs were loaded into the pore channels of the amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H composite, leading to reduced channel diameter and pore volume.

Variations in the FL response of amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H toward DNP at different pH, concentrations, and absorption times were investigated. The change in FL intensity of the amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H composite at different pH was evaluated. The quenching efficiency was highest at pH 7 (Fig. S6). The response time and amount of amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H are also key factors for practical applications. An amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H concentration of 40 μg mL−1 was found to be the optimum concentration, and the optimum time for generating a response was 10 min. This short response time could be attributed to the mesoporous structure and the strong driving forces for the adsorption of DNP on amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H.

Generally, metal ions and nitrophenol analogs can quench the FL of amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H. Thus, the selectivity of the fluorescent amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H sensing system was assessed. Fig. 6 shows the effects of other metal ions such as Fe3+, Cr3+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Ag+, K+, Na+, and Li+ and nitrophenol analogs other than DNP (e.g., PHE, 4-NP, and TNP) on the FL response of amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H under the same experimental conditions. The FL response (I 0/I–1) of amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H toward DNP (>1.31) was significantly higher than that toward others metal ions and nitrophenol analogs (<0.3), except for 4-NP, TNP, and Fe3+. These three species displayed slight FL quenching due to their stronger interactions with the free –NH2 groups of the amino-CQDs, although the quenching was insignificant. No obvious changes in the FL intensity were observed in the presence of PHE or the other metal ions. The high selectivity was ascribed to the selective sensing of DNP by the amino-CQDs, which led to the selective adsorption of DNP by amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H. To demonstrate the high selectivity, comparative experiments were performed (Fig. S7). No FL quenching was observed when different phenol analogs were added to the parent amino-CQDs solution (at a fixed concentration). However, the FL quenching efficiency of TNP was highest among the four phenol analogs investigated.

To understand the sensing mechanism of DNP by the synthesized amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3 composite, the UV–vis absorption of DNP and FL emission of amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H were examined (Fig. S8). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of DNP overlapped partly with the FL emission spectrum of amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H. Amino-CQDs in the composite and DNP acted as the donor and acceptor, respectively. The partial overlap indicated an underlying electron transfer process. Since TNP has more –NO2 groups compared to DNP, 4-NP, and phenol, electrons are more easily transferred from the –NH2 groups on the amino-CQDs surface to its benzene ring, resulting in greater FL quenching efficiency.

The DNP adsorption uptake by amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H was 96.1 mg g−1 at a DNP concentration of 20 ppm, and the adsorption uptakes for TNP and 4-NP were 13.1 and 3.8 mg g−1, respectively. The larger adsorptivity of the amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H composite for DNP is probably due to differences in the size and hydrogen bonding interactions of TNP and 4-NP. Although the FL quenching efficiency of DNP in the parent amino-CQDs solution was lower than that of TNP, the adsorption uptake of DNP on the amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H composite was significantly higher than that of TNP. Therefore, amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H exhibited specific recognition toward DNP. Since there are no recognition sites for metal ions in the amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H composite, quenching by metal ions was effectively prevented. Therefore, amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H is a potential FL material for detecting DNP with high selectivity.

The FL spectrum of amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H changed significantly with increasing amounts of DNP. The intensity of the emission peak at 439.6 nm gradually decreased, i.e. the FL quenching efficiency of amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H gradually increased. This suggested a dose-dependent interaction between the composite and DNP (Fig. 7 ). Fig. 7b shows a linear plot of the FL emission intensity ratio (I 0/I) vs. concentration (C). The fitting equation for DNP in the concentration range 0–40 μM is y = 0.1393x + 0.1531. The corresponding R 2 value (0.9918) suggests a good linear relationship, with K sv = 0.1393 μM−1. The detection limit of amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H for DNP was calculated to be 4.5 nM (S/N = 3), which is comparable to or even higher than previously reported luminescence sensors [37–39]. This indicates that the amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H composite is highly sensitive toward DNP. This can be attributed to the superior selective absorption of the MOF toward DNP, which is actually driven by the effective recognition of the FL amino-CQDs by the composite.

In this study, mesostructured sulfo-modified MIL-101-SO3H was prepared using a facile solvothermal method. Amino-CQDs were encapsulated inside MIL-101-SO3H by hydrogen bonding interactions between the –NH2 groups of the amino-CQDs and -SO3H groups of the MOF skeleton, to afford a fluorescent amino-CQDs@MIL-101-SO3H sensor. The sensor exhibited the highly sensitive and selective detection of DNP, compared to the parent amino-CQDs, in solution due to the superior selective adsorption of DNP in the pore cavity of the MOF via hydrogen bonding interactions between the –NO2 groups of DNP and the –SO3H groups of the MOF skeleton. This induced charge transfer between DNP and the functional amino-CQDs in the recognition sites. This study will provide a new platform for the preparation of FL-based MOF sensors for applications in the fields of chemical and toxic pollutant sensing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation, China (Grant No. ZR2018LB05), a project of the Shandong Province Higher Educational Science and Technology Program (Grant No. J16LC04), and the Natural Science Foundation of Linyi University (Grant No. LYDX2016BS072).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ica.2021.120276.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
